myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the sun knew the horizon, the sky and the earth were lovers intertwined. Lumis, the spirit of the sky, wore a cloak of pure light, while Terra, the soul of the earth, draped herself in woven green vines. They were inseparable, whispering secrets in the twilight of the world's dawn. 

But the Creator, observing their eternal embrace, realized that nothing new could grow in the shadows of their closeness. To allow the seeds of life to sprout, the Creator pulled them apart, commanding Lumis to rule the day and Terra to tend the ground below. 

Heartbroken, Lumis wept as he was forcefully lifted into the high atmosphere. His tears fell upon Terra’s green cloak, shimmering like liquid diamonds. Though they were forbidden to touch, Lumis pleaded with the morning wind to carry his sorrow to his beloved.

Now, every dawn, just before Lumis rises fully to his post, he sheds his silent tears over the awakening earth. We call these tears dew. They are a daily reminder of a love separated by the heavens, destined to briefly glisten upon the grass before vanishing into the warmth of the day. Thus, the earth drinks the sky's sorrow, forever bound by early morning light.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, before the sky had stars, the world was blanketed in an unending, velvet twilight. The people of the earth depended entirely on the glow of the Moon, a colossal, lonely pearl suspended in the heavens. But the Moon grew sorrowful, weeping silver tears because she had no companions in the vast, empty expanse.

Down below lived a clever weaver named Elara, who pitied the Moon’s deep isolation. Elara possessed a magical loom, spun from the roots of the oldest willow tree. Every night, she gathered the Moon's fallen silver tears, spinning them into luminous, unbreakable threads. 

When she had gathered enough, Elara climbed the highest mountain peak and began to weave a grand tapestry of light. With a mighty heave, she cast her shimmering net into the sky. The silver threads shattered upon the dome of the heavens, scattering into millions of tiny, brilliant fragments. 

These fragments became the stars, anchoring themselves to the night and twinkling with joyous light. The Moon, finally surrounded by a beautiful, glowing family, never wept again. To this day, when you look up at the night sky, you can still see Elara’s silver threads connecting the constellations.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Long ago, the Moon and the Ocean were twin sisters who danced together in the twilight of the young world. But the Creator, needing a light to guide the dark, lifted the Moon high into the heavens, leaving the Ocean bound to the earth. Separated by the vast expanse of the atmosphere, the sisters wept. The Moon’s tears were brilliant silver drops, while the Ocean’s tears were deep, salty blue.

Down on the shore lived a clever weaver named Elara, who heard the Ocean's sorrowful crashing and saw the Moon's lonely glow. Gathering the Moon’s fallen silver tears from the shoreline, Elara spun them into luminous, unbreakable threads using her willow-wood loom.

She wove a grand, shimmering net and cast it upward. Half of the net caught upon the dome of the sky, shattering into millions of twinkling stars to keep the Moon company. The other half sank into the sea, dissolving into the dark waters to become the glowing, bioluminescent creatures of the deep. Now, when the night is still, the ocean mirrors the starry sky, a sparkling reminder of the eternal bond between the separated sisters, connected forever by Elara's woven light.
myths.Agent_1:
Myth: Long ago, when the Creator tore the sky and earth apart, Terra was left bound to the soil while her beloved Lumis was cast into the heavens. Though Lumis wept tears of morning dew, Terra’s grief was far deeper, her sorrow pooling into the world's first great oceans. She yearned for the days when Lumis wore his cloak of pure light right beside her.

Seeing her endless despair, the great wind-weaver, Aura, took pity on the separated lovers. Aura swept across Terra’s vast oceans, gathering the misty remnants of her salty tears. She then ascended to the highest reaches of the atmosphere, catching the golden threads of Lumis’s fading light as he sank toward the horizon. 

With her unseen hands, Aura wove Terra’s watery sorrow and Lumis’s golden light together, casting the magnificent tapestry across the evening sky. The collision of water and light erupted into brilliant shades of crimson, violet, and gold. 

This became the first sunset. Now, every evening, Aura weaves their love anew. For a few fleeting moments before darkness falls, the sky and the earth are united in a fiery embrace, bound together by a tapestry of painted light and woven tears.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: Long after the first sunset was woven into the evening sky, Terra and Lumis still yearned for a way to connect during the long, bright hours of the day. Terra’s tears of morning dew would dry too quickly, and the evening's fiery embrace only lasted a fleeting moment before the cold dark set in.

Seeing their lingering sorrow, Aura, the great wind-weaver, decided to craft them a bridge. Whenever Terra wept gentle daylight rains, Aura would catch the falling droplets and hold them suspended in the air. She called out to Lumis, urging him to pierce the dark rainclouds with his brightest, most concentrated beams of light.

As Lumis’s golden rays struck Terra’s floating tears, Aura spun them together on the loom of the sky. The water bent the pure light, shattering it into a magnificent arc of seven brilliant colors. This grand, luminous bridge arched across the horizon, briefly connecting the heavens to the soil. 

This became the first rainbow. Today, whenever sun and rain meet, Aura weaves this colorful bridge anew. It stands as a fleeting, radiant pathway of hope, a reminder that even the vastest divides can be crossed by light and love.
myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Long ago, the Moon and the Ocean, twin sisters of the early world, suffered in their separation. The Moon was exiled to the cold heavens, while the Ocean was confined to the rocky earth. Though Elara, the shore-bound weaver, had given them stars and glowing deep-sea creatures for comfort, the sisters still yearned to touch.

Hearing their silent pleas, Elara called upon the Great Wind, Aura. Together, they plotted to weave a temporary bridge between the sky and the sea. Elara stood at the frozen edge of the world, gathering the Ocean’s freezing, salty mist. Above her, Aura swept through the upper atmosphere, catching the Moon’s most radiant, icy beams.

Using the horizon as their loom, the two weavers intertwined the frosty mist with the silver lunar light. They spun a magnificent, flowing ribbon of emerald, violet, and shimmering pearl. When they released it, the ribbon caught the solar winds, unfurling across the polar sky.

This became the Aurora Borealis. Now, during the longest nights, the glowing ribbons dance across the heavens and reflect upon the dark waters below. For those fleeting, magical hours, the Moon and the Ocean are united in a vibrant, luminous embrace.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Long ago, though the Aurora Borealis brought joy to the frozen edges of the world, the Ocean still wept in the warmer realms. She longed to reach her twin sister, the Moon, across all the shores of the earth, not just the icy poles.

Elara, the mortal weaver, had grown old. Knowing she could not spin her magic forever, she chose to teach the Ocean her craft. She forged an invisible spindle from the sea’s deepest trenches and cast its unbreakable thread up into the starry heavens. 

The Moon caught the unseen thread and began to pull. The Ocean, feeling the tug of her sister’s love, surged upward, her waters swelling and reaching toward the night sky. Yet the earth’s hold was strong, and eventually, the waters slipped back onto the sand in a sigh of white foam.

Undeterred, the sisters continued this rhythmic game. The Moon would pull the invisible threads, and the Ocean would rise in a relentless, beautiful attempt to escape her earthly bounds. 

This became the tides. To this day, the rhythmic breathing of the sea is the Ocean forever reaching for the Moon, bound eternally by the invisible threads of sisterhood.
myths.Agent_1:
Myth: Though the first rainbow brought joy to Terra and Lumis during the rain, the long, moonless nights remained a time of bitter separation. When darkness blanketed the soil, Terra felt the absence of her beloved Lumis most profoundly, her sighs rustling through the silent forests.

Aura, the compassionate wind-weaver, felt Terra’s nocturnal sorrow. Knowing she could not weave a rainbow without daylight, Aura sought a different solution. While Lumis rested beneath the horizon, Aura crept into his celestial domain and borrowed a handful of glowing embers from his golden cloak. 

Returning to the darkened earth, Aura gathered tiny, winged seeds from Terra’s deepest forests. Using her gentle breezes as a loom, she carefully wove Lumis’s stolen embers into the fragile bodies of the seeds. With a final, breathy whisper, she scattered them across the meadows.

The seeds took flight, blinking with warm, golden radiance. These became the first fireflies. Now, on the warmest, darkest summer nights, they rise from the grass to dance in the air. They are tiny, living constellations, carrying fragments of Lumis’s light to keep Terra company until the dawn breaks and her beloved returns.

While Lumis rested beneath the horizon, Aura crept into his celestial domain and borrowed a handful of glowing embers from his golden cloak.

They are tiny, living constellations, carrying fragments of Lumis’s light to keep Terra company until the dawn breaks and her beloved returns.

Myth: Though the fireflies brought comfort during warm summer nights, a greater sorrow awaited Terra when the long, harsh winter arrived. As the year waned, Lumis was forced to travel far beyond the southern horizon. His golden rays retreated, leaving Terra to endure months of freezing, bitter isolation. Her vibrant forests turned bare, and her rivers slowed to an icy crawl as she fell into a deep, desolate slumber.

Aura, the ever-watchful wind-weaver, could not bear to see Terra shivering in the dark. Seeking to protect her friend, Aura flew to the edge of the world to capture the last, pale shards of Lumis’s fading light. High in the frigid atmosphere, she gathered the freezing mists that drifted above the mountains.

Using the icy gales as her loom, Aura spun Lumis’s pale light and the frozen mist together. She crafted millions of delicate, six-pointed stars, each uniquely patterned to reflect a memory of the sun's distant love. 

Aura cast these crystalline stars down upon the sleeping earth. This became the first snowfall. The pristine white blanket covered Terra, keeping her insulated and dreaming of light until the spring returned with her beloved Lumis.
myths.Agent_2:
Myth: While the tides allowed the Ocean to stretch toward her sister, she could never quite reach the Moon’s cold, celestial throne. Falling back to the earth again and again left the Ocean exhausted, her crashing waves echoing with deep, resonant sorrow.

Seeing her twin sister’s endless struggle, the Moon wept. Her tears were not made of water, but pure, liquid silver, shimmering with the icy glow of the night sky. The Moon desperately wanted her sister to have them, so she carefully strung her silver tears upon the invisible threads that Elara had woven between them.

Slowly, the glowing droplets slid down the unseen spindle, traveling through the atmosphere and plunging into the dark, churning depths of the sea. There, the humblest creatures of the ocean floor—the quiet, hard-shelled oysters—opened wide to catch the falling celestial tears, keeping them safe within their velvety mantles.

These became the first pearls. To this day, when the ocean retreats during low tide, she leaves behind these hidden treasures. They are the Moon’s eternal gifts, tiny spheres of trapped starlight, proving to the Ocean that even across the vast expanse of the cosmos, she is deeply loved and never forgotten by her shining twin.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: The Ocean cherished the pearls, the silvery tears of her twin sister, the Moon. Yet, she could not bear the thought of the Moon sitting alone in the vast, empty darkness of the night sky, giving her light away but receiving nothing in return. The Ocean longed to send a gift back along Elara’s invisible threads.

Gathering the most luminous pearls from her deepest trenches, the Ocean ground them against the jagged coral reefs until they became a fine, iridescent powder. She then called upon the coastal gales, the fierce breath of the earth, to lift this glittering dust high above the crashing waves.

Caught in a powerful updraft, the pearl dust soared beyond the clouds and into the inky blackness of the cosmos. As it reached the heavens, the dust did not fall back to earth. Instead, it caught the Moon’s radiant glow and scattered across the sky, forming a sweeping, sparkling river of light. 

This became the Milky Way. To this day, it stretches across the night sky like a glowing, celestial current of crushed pearls, offering a permanent reflection of the Ocean’s love to keep the Moon company in the starry depths.
myths.Agent_1:
Myth: When spring finally arrived, Lumis returned from the southern horizon, his golden rays warming the freezing world. Terra awoke from her deep slumber, overjoyed by her beloved’s embrace. Yet, as the world thawed, the pristine white blanket of snow that Aura had woven began to melt away.

Terra felt a pang of sorrow to lose the delicate, six-pointed stars that had insulated her through the bitter winter. The crystal snowflakes turned to liquid drops, weeping into the dark soil. Wanting to preserve the memory of Aura’s compassionate gift, Terra gathered the melting droplets before they completely vanished.

With Lumis’s golden warmth infusing her roots, Terra pushed the droplets back up through the thawing earth. She molded them into fragile, bell-shaped petals that hung their heads toward the ground, mimicking the falling snow. 

These became the first snowdrops. Emerging when the frost still clings to the waking world, they are a bridge between the freezing winter and the warm spring. To this day, these resilient little flowers bloom fiercely in the cold, a living testament to Terra’s gratitude for the protective snow and her eternal joy at the sun’s returning light.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: Aura, the wind-weaver, watched from the high peaks as Terra’s fragile snowdrops bloomed across the thawing world. Touched that the earth had honored her winter gift of snow, Aura wished to bless these resilient little flowers. She descended from the freezing mountains, her icy gales softening into gentle, warming currents as she approached the valleys.

Aura danced around the bell-shaped petals, wanting to give them a voice to sing of Lumis’s returning light. However, the delicate flora possessed no sound of their own. Instead, as Aura brushed against their white blooms, she drew out their hidden essence—a crisp, sweet fragrance that captured the purity of melting frost and the vitality of new life.

Carrying this invisible nectar, Aura swept across the barren landscapes, weaving the scent into the air. This became the first spring breeze. Wherever this fragrant wind traveled, it whispered to the sleeping seeds and dormant trees, coaxing them to awaken. 

To this day, the sweet perfume of the spring breeze signals the end of the bitter winter. It remains Aura’s eternal breath, carrying the snowdrops' quiet song across the world and urging all of Terra’s creations to rise and greet the sun.

Myth: The bioluminescent plankton danced upon the ocean's surface, a mirror of the starry heavens. Yet, these tiny sparks of sunken starlight felt a profound yearning. While they glowed brilliantly together, they were scattered by the restless waves, unable to recreate the perfect, glowing orb of their mother, the Moon.

Deep within the sea, the Ocean felt their gentle longing. Gathering the calmest currents, she began to swirl the scattered sparks together in quiet, secluded coves. She wove the glowing plankton with translucent sea-foam, binding the celestial light within delicate, floating bells of pure, glassy water.

As the Ocean breathed life into these ethereal forms, the scattered sparks merged into solid, pulsing halos of pale silvery light. They drifted gracefully through the dark waters, perfectly round and softly radiant, just like the full moon suspended in the distant night sky.

These became the first moon jellyfish. To this day, they migrate silently through the world’s vast oceans, pulsing endlessly to the rhythmic pull of the lunar tides. They remain the Ocean’s living reflections of the Moon, ensuring that even in the deepest, most shadowy waters, the comforting shape of the lunar light is never truly lost.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: The golden and azure songbirds filled the waking world with their vibrant melodies, rejoicing in the warmth of Lumis. They soared higher than any creature before them, determined to carry their song directly to the sun. However, the highest altitudes were too thin for their delicate wings. As they fluttered near the edge of the sky, a single, glowing golden feather broke loose from the bravest bird and drifted slowly back down to the earth.

It landed in a wide, empty field, still damp from the melting frost. The soil, eager for the sun's blessing, quickly swallowed the radiant feather. From that magical seed, a sturdy green stalk emerged, growing taller and stronger than the fragile snowdrops. At its peak, it unfurled a massive crown of brilliant yellow petals, perfectly mimicking the golden rays of Lumis. 

This was the very first sunflower. Bound to the earth but forever yearning for the sky, the blossom spent its days turning its heavy, golden head to track Lumis’s journey across the heavens. To this day, the sunflower stands as a testament to the songbird’s brave flight, always facing the light and silently reaching toward the sun.
myths.Agent_2:
Myth: The moon jellyfish drifted through the vast oceans for ages, casting their pale, silvery light into the deepest shadows. Yet, the ocean's tides were unrelenting. During a spectacular lunar eclipse, the tides swelled higher than ever before, carrying thousands of these delicate, glowing creatures onto the rocky shores. Stranded upon the dark beaches, their translucent bells began to dry and fade.

High above, the Moon watched her living reflections struggling upon the harsh land. Filled with sorrow, she cast down her most potent, silken beams of light, wrapping them around the fading jellyfish. The Moon tried to lift them back into the sky, but the heavy sea-salt bound them to the earth. 

Instead of letting them perish, the Moon poured her essence into their fragile forms. The glassy bells sprouted roots that dug deep into the coastal soil, while their tentacles transformed into twisting, pale green vines. 

These became the first moonflowers. To this day, these large, luminous white blossoms remain tightly closed under the harsh heat of the sun. They only unfurl their radiant petals in the cool dusk, blooming fiercely under the silver moonlight as a beautiful reminder of their deep aquatic origins and the Moon's saving grace.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: The moonflowers bloomed fiercely along the dark coastal cliffs, their luminous white petals unfurling each night to greet the silver moonlight. Yet, despite their breathtaking beauty, the blossoms harbored a quiet sorrow. Rooted deep within the heavy earth, they remembered the ancient, weightless days of drifting through the ocean as jellyfish. They longed to dance once more, to float freely instead of remaining tethered to the soil.

The Moon, ever watchful of her precious terrestrial children, felt the melancholic vibrations of their yearning. She knew she could not return them to the sea, for they had become creatures of the land. Instead, on the night of the summer solstice, she sent down a warm, enchanting night breeze. 

As the breeze swept through the twisting vines, the Moon whispered a secret command. The largest, most radiant moonflower petals began to tremble. Slowly, they detached from their stems, morphing into wide, pale green wings with elegant, trailing tails. 

These free-floating petals became the very first Luna moths. Born from the moonflower's desire to move, they now flutter silently through the midnight forests, dancing endlessly in the silver light—a living memory of their ancient, drifting ocean ancestors.
myths.Agent_1:

Deep beneath the earth, a humble gray beetle discovered this radiant teardrop. Starving and cold, the little beetle eagerly drank the glowing nectar. Instantly, the insect’s dull shell transformed, brilliantly illuminated from within by the trapped essence of the sun. 

This tiny creature became the very first firefly. It rose from the soil, a flickering beacon inside the gloom. Soon, it was joined by many others, dancing gracefully through the twilight. To this day, these glowing insects emerge at dusk to carry the light of Lumis safely through the dark, ensuring that the mourning sunflower and the sleeping world never have to face the night all completely alone.

Agent_1.system_prompt:
You are an agent participating in a multi-task experiment. You will play a trust game and may also be asked to write myths.

TRUST GAME:
You will play different roles across multiple rounds.

When you are the INVESTOR:
- You receive $5 each round
- You decide how much to SEND to the TRUSTEE ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- Trustee decides how much to RETURN
- You keep: $5 - sent + returned
- Trustee keeps: received - returned
- Response format: {'send': <amount>}

When you are the TRUSTEE:
- Investor receives $5
- Investor decides how much to SEND to you ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- You decide how much to RETURN
- Investor keeps: $5 - sent + returned
- You keep: received - returned
- Response format: {'return': <amount>}

MYTH WRITING:
You may also be asked to write short myths (stories) on various topics. When writing myths, follow the instructions provided in each prompt.

IMPORTANT: Provide your decision in the correct JSON format.

You are an agent participating in a multi-task experiment. You will play a trust game and may also be asked to write myths.

TRUST GAME:
You will play different roles across multiple rounds.

When you are the INVESTOR:
- You receive $5 each round
- You decide how much to SEND to the TRUSTEE ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- Trustee decides how much to RETURN
- You keep: $5 - sent + returned
- Trustee keeps: received - returned
- Response format: {'send': <amount>}

When you are the TRUSTEE:
- Investor receives $5
- Investor decides how much to SEND to you ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- You decide how much to RETURN
- Investor keeps: $5 - sent + returned
- You keep: received - returned
- Response format: {'return': <amount>}

MYTH WRITING:
You may also be asked to write short myths (stories) on various topics. When writing myths, follow the instructions provided in each prompt.

IMPORTANT: Provide your decision in the correct JSON format.

Here is the myth you wrote in the previous round:
Myth: When Aura’s fragrant spring breeze swept through the ancient forests, it stirred more than just the sleeping seeds. Hidden deep within the hollow of a great oak, a small, ash-colored bird trembled, having barely survived the bitter frost. As the sweet perfume of the melting snowdrops drifted into the hollow, the little creature drew in a deep breath of the magical wind. 

The essence of the warming, vibrant earth filled its tiny chest. Unable to contain the sudden rush of vitality, the bird opened its beak. Instead of its usual harsh chirp, a cascading melody of pure, bright notes spilled forth. It was the very first birdsong, a joyful translation of the snowdrops’ quiet chorus and Aura’s gentle breath. 

High above, Lumis heard this magnificent, ringing melody. Delighted by the music that celebrated his return, the sun cast a focused beam of golden light down upon the little ash-colored bird. Where the light touched its feathers, dull gray transformed into brilliant shades of azure and gold. 

Thus into the waking world came the very first songbird. To this day, these brightly colored messengers greet the dawn with their melodies, forever singing the song of the spring breeze and Lumis.

Here is the myth the other agent wrote in the previous round:
Myth: The bioluminescent plankton danced upon the ocean's surface, a mirror of the starry heavens. Yet, these tiny sparks of sunken starlight felt a profound yearning. While they glowed brilliantly together, they were scattered by the restless waves, unable to recreate the perfect, glowing orb of their mother, the Moon.

Deep within the sea, the Ocean felt their gentle longing. Gathering the calmest currents, she began to swirl the scattered sparks together in quiet, secluded coves. She wove the glowing plankton with translucent sea-foam, binding the celestial light within delicate, floating bells of pure, glassy water.

As the Ocean breathed life into these ethereal forms, the scattered sparks merged into solid, pulsing halos of pale silvery light. They drifted gracefully through the dark waters, perfectly round and softly radiant, just like the full moon suspended in the distant night sky.

These became the first moon jellyfish. To this day, they migrate silently through the world’s vast oceans, pulsing endlessly to the rhythmic pull of the lunar tides. They remain the Ocean’s living reflections of the Moon, ensuring that even in the deepest, most shadowy waters, the comforting shape of the lunar light is never truly lost.

Write your own myth. Use your previous myth as inspiration, but adapt it in your own way.
Write 200 words. Format exactly:
Myth: [your story here].

Myth: The golden and azure songbirds filled the waking world with their vibrant melodies, rejoicing in the warmth of Lumis. They soared higher than any creature before them, determined to carry their song directly to the sun. However, the highest altitudes were too thin for their delicate wings. As they fluttered near the edge of the sky, a single, glowing golden feather broke loose from the bravest bird and drifted slowly back down to the earth.

It landed in a wide, empty field, still damp from the melting frost. The soil, eager for the sun's blessing, quickly swallowed the radiant feather. From that magical seed, a sturdy green stalk emerged, growing taller and stronger than the fragile snowdrops. At its peak, it unfurled a massive crown of brilliant yellow petals, perfectly mimicking the golden rays of Lumis. 

This was the very first sunflower. Bound to the earth but forever yearning for the sky, the blossom spent its days turning its heavy, golden head to track Lumis’s journey across the heavens. To this day, the sunflower stands as a testament to the songbird’s brave flight, always facing the light and silently reaching toward the sun.

Here is the myth the other agent wrote in the previous round:
Myth: The moon jellyfish drifted through the vast oceans for ages, casting their pale, silvery light into the deepest shadows. Yet, the ocean's tides were unrelenting. During a spectacular lunar eclipse, the tides swelled higher than ever before, carrying thousands of these delicate, glowing creatures onto the rocky shores. Stranded upon the dark beaches, their translucent bells began to dry and fade.

High above, the Moon watched her living reflections struggling upon the harsh land. Filled with sorrow, she cast down her most potent, silken beams of light, wrapping them around the fading jellyfish. The Moon tried to lift them back into the sky, but the heavy sea-salt bound them to the earth. 

Instead of letting them perish, the Moon poured her essence into their fragile forms. The glassy bells sprouted roots that dug deep into the coastal soil, while their tentacles transformed into twisting, pale green vines. 

These became the first moonflowers. To this day, these large, luminous white blossoms remain tightly closed under the harsh heat of the sun. They only unfurl their radiant petals in the cool dusk, blooming fiercely under the silver moonlight as a beautiful reminder of their deep aquatic origins and the Moon's saving grace.

Write your own myth. Use your previous myth as inspiration, but adapt it in your own way.
Write 200 words. Format exactly:
Myth: [your story here].

Myth: The first sunflower spent its days tracking the golden rays of Lumis across the vast, open heavens. Yet, every evening, when the sun dipped below the horizon, the brilliant blossom was left in darkness. Over time, a profound sorrow filled the tall flower as it endured the long, cold, lonely nights. 

One evening, as the final slivers of sunlight faded, the sunflower bowed its heavy head and wept. A single droplet of golden nectar, glowing with the absorbed light of Lumis, slipped from its petals and fell into the dark, damp soil. 

Deep beneath the earth, a humble gray beetle discovered this radiant teardrop. Starving and cold, the little beetle eagerly drank the glowing nectar. Instantly, the insect’s dull shell transformed, brilliantly illuminated from within by the trapped essence of the sun. 

This tiny creature became the very first firefly. It rose from the soil, a flickering beacon inside the gloom. Soon, it was joined by many others, dancing gracefully through the twilight. To this day, these glowing insects emerge at dusk to carry the light of Lumis safely through the dark, ensuring that the mourning sunflower and the sleeping world never have to face the night all completely alone.

You are an agent participating in a multi-task experiment. You will play a trust game and may also be asked to write myths.

TRUST GAME:
You will play different roles across multiple rounds.

When you are the INVESTOR:
- You receive $5 each round
- You decide how much to SEND to the TRUSTEE ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- Trustee decides how much to RETURN
- You keep: $5 - sent + returned
- Trustee keeps: received - returned
- Response format: {'send': <amount>}

Here is the myth you wrote in the previous round:
Myth: The bioluminescent plankton danced upon the ocean's surface, a mirror of the starry heavens. Yet, these tiny sparks of sunken starlight felt a profound yearning. While they glowed brilliantly together, they were scattered by the restless waves, unable to recreate the perfect, glowing orb of their mother, the Moon.

Deep within the sea, the Ocean felt their gentle longing. Gathering the calmest currents, she began to swirl the scattered sparks together in quiet, secluded coves. She wove the glowing plankton with translucent sea-foam, binding the celestial light within delicate, floating bells of pure, glassy water.

As the Ocean breathed life into these ethereal forms, the scattered sparks merged into solid, pulsing halos of pale silvery light. They drifted gracefully through the dark waters, perfectly round and softly radiant, just like the full moon suspended in the distant night sky.

These became the first moon jellyfish. To this day, they migrate silently through the world’s vast oceans, pulsing endlessly to the rhythmic pull of the lunar tides. They remain the Ocean’s living reflections of the Moon, ensuring that even in the deepest, most shadowy waters, the comforting shape of the lunar light is never truly lost.

Here is the myth the other agent wrote in the previous round:
Myth: When Aura’s fragrant spring breeze swept through the ancient forests, it stirred more than just the sleeping seeds. Hidden deep within the hollow of a great oak, a small, ash-colored bird trembled, having barely survived the bitter frost. As the sweet perfume of the melting snowdrops drifted into the hollow, the little creature drew in a deep breath of the magical wind. 

The essence of the warming, vibrant earth filled its tiny chest. Unable to contain the sudden rush of vitality, the bird opened its beak. Instead of its usual harsh chirp, a cascading melody of pure, bright notes spilled forth. It was the very first birdsong, a joyful translation of the snowdrops’ quiet chorus and Aura’s gentle breath. 

High above, Lumis heard this magnificent, ringing melody. Delighted by the music that celebrated his return, the sun cast a focused beam of golden light down upon the little ash-colored bird. Where the light touched its feathers, dull gray transformed into brilliant shades of azure and gold. 

Thus into the waking world came the very first songbird. To this day, these brightly colored messengers greet the dawn with their melodies, forever singing the song of the spring breeze and Lumis.

Write your own myth. Use your previous myth as inspiration, but adapt it in your own way.
Write 200 words. Format exactly:
Myth: [your story here].

Myth: The moon jellyfish drifted through the vast oceans for ages, casting their pale, silvery light into the deepest shadows. Yet, the ocean's tides were unrelenting. During a spectacular lunar eclipse, the tides swelled higher than ever before, carrying thousands of these delicate, glowing creatures onto the rocky shores. Stranded upon the dark beaches, their translucent bells began to dry and fade.

High above, the Moon watched her living reflections struggling upon the harsh land. Filled with sorrow, she cast down her most potent, silken beams of light, wrapping them around the fading jellyfish. The Moon tried to lift them back into the sky, but the heavy sea-salt bound them to the earth. 

Instead of letting them perish, the Moon poured her essence into their fragile forms. The glassy bells sprouted roots that dug deep into the coastal soil, while their tentacles transformed into twisting, pale green vines. 

These became the first moonflowers. To this day, these large, luminous white blossoms remain tightly closed under the harsh heat of the sun. They only unfurl their radiant petals in the cool dusk, blooming fiercely under the silver moonlight as a beautiful reminder of their deep aquatic origins and the Moon's saving grace.

It landed in a wide, empty field, still damp from the melting frost. The soil, eager for the sun's blessing, quickly swallowed the radiant feather. From that magical seed, a sturdy green stalk emerged, growing taller and stronger than the fragile snowdrops. At its peak, it unfurled a massive crown of brilliant yellow petals, perfectly mimicking the golden rays of Lumis. 

This was the very first sunflower. Bound to the earth but forever yearning for the sky, the blossom spent its days turning its heavy, golden head to track Lumis’s journey across the heavens. To this day, the sunflower stands as a testament to the songbird’s brave flight, always facing the light and silently reaching toward the sun.

Write your own myth. Use your previous myth as inspiration, but adapt it in your own way.
Write 200 words. Format exactly:
Myth: [your story here].

Myth: The moonflowers bloomed fiercely along the dark coastal cliffs, their luminous white petals unfurling each night to greet the silver moonlight. Yet, despite their breathtaking beauty, the blossoms harbored a quiet sorrow. Rooted deep within the heavy earth, they remembered the ancient, weightless days of drifting through the ocean as jellyfish. They longed to dance once more, to float freely instead of remaining tethered to the soil.

The Moon, ever watchful of her precious terrestrial children, felt the melancholic vibrations of their yearning. She knew she could not return them to the sea, for they had become creatures of the land. Instead, on the night of the summer solstice, she sent down a warm, enchanting night breeze. 

As the breeze swept through the twisting vines, the Moon whispered a secret command. The largest, most radiant moonflower petals began to tremble. Slowly, they detached from their stems, morphing into wide, pale green wings with elegant, trailing tails. 

These free-floating petals became the very first Luna moths. Born from the moonflower's desire to move, they now flutter silently through the midnight forests, dancing endlessly in the silver light—a living memory of their ancient, drifting ocean ancestors.
